question: What is your RTO and RPO? How do you ensure business continuity during disasters?
title: Business Continuity, Disaster Recovery Objectives (RTO/RPO), and Resilience Testing
bullets: Define RTO (time to restore service) and RPO (acceptable data loss window) with clear values and scope
Describe DR architecture (multi-region, active-active/active-passive, failover mechanisms, backups)
Explain backup strategy (frequency, retention, geographic diversity, point-in-time recovery)
Provide evidence of DR testing (frequency, last test date, results, runbooks, and improvements)
suggested_inputs: Business Continuity Plan (BCP) and Disaster Recovery (DR) runbooks
DR test reports (latest results, issues, remediation actions)

good.text: RTO: [e.g., 2 hours], RPO: [e.g., 5 minutes]. DR architecture uses multi-region replication with automated failover. Backups are continuous with point-in-time recovery. DR is tested at least annually (or quarterly), with last test date and results documented. Runbooks are maintained and updated.

We operate an active-active multi-region architecture, minimizing downtime without manual failover. Data is continuously replicated, achieving near-zero RPO. Failures are handled transparently with health checks and traffic routing.
Backups are taken continuously or at frequent intervals, stored in geographically separate regions, and retained per policy. Restoration is regularly validated through drills and integrity checks.
We conduct regular DR exercises with documented outcomes, identified gaps, and remediation actions. Results are tracked, and runbooks are updated to improve resilience over time.

partial.text: We maintain backups and recovery processes, but do not clearly define RTO/RPO targets.
We rely on our cloud provider for resilience, but our own DR responsibilities and controls are not fully detailed.
DR testing is performed occasionally, but results, frequency, or improvements are not formally documented.
We have failover and backup mechanisms, but architecture, geographic distribution, and recovery processes are not clearly defined.

bad.text: We do not define RTO or RPO for our services.
We have backups, but no defined recovery objectives or tested DR processes.
Disaster recovery is handled entirely by our cloud provider.
We have never needed to use or test our disaster recovery plan.

follow_ups: Walk through a disaster scenario: primary region fails—what happens step-by-step until full recovery?
Provide the latest DR test report, including issues identified and remediation actions.
If your entire primary infrastructure is unavailable, how can we access our data and how quickly?
What are your backup retention periods and geographic distribution of backups?
Provide runbooks for common failure scenarios and evidence they are regularly updated.
